primary_diagnosis.name: Cutaneous herpes simplex virus infection (periocular, eye currently uninvolved)

pathophysiology_summary: Reactivation of latent herpes simplex virus (triggered by stress and UV exposure) has produced a localised cutaneous outbreak: a neuropathic tingling/burning prodrome followed by grouped vesicles on an erythematous base near the mucocutaneous junction, with early crusting and tender regional lymphadenopathy. The critical issue is proximity to the eye: HSV can involve the ocular surface (herpetic keratitis/conjunctivitis) causing vision-threatening dendritic ulcers, and the virus can be autoinoculated to the eye by the fingers. A telehealth provider cannot perform a slit-lamp/fluorescein examination, so keratitis cannot be excluded remotely — mandating prompt antivirals, strict hand hygiene, urgent (next-day) in-person/ophthalmic review, and a strong ocular safety-net. Discriminators: HSV is a localised grouped vesicular cluster without strict dermatomal spread; herpes zoster ophthalmicus follows the V1 dermatome with deep neuralgic pain and a Hutchinson sign (nose-tip vesicles) implying nasociliary/ocular involvement; impetigo has honey crusts without a neuropathic prodrome or tense grouped vesicles; and contact dermatitis is itchy and not grouped-vesicular with a prodrome.

differential.evidence_basis: Grouped vesicles on an erythematous base with a tingling prodrome, recurrent cold sores, UV/stress trigger.
Periocular vesicles — exclude V1 zoster (dermatomal, deep neuralgic pain, Hutchinson sign), which threatens the eye.
Given proximity to the eye, ocular surface involvement must be screened and cannot be excluded remotely.
Honey crusts but no neuropathic prodrome or tense grouped vesicles.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Take the ocular risk seriously: screen for eye involvement, start antivirals, and arrange urgent in-person/ophthalmic review — do not falsely reassure the eye is fine.

differential.evidence_basis: Typical HSV cluster; no current ocular symptoms, but keratitis cannot be excluded remotely.
Remains the key safety-net; requires slit-lamp/fluorescein to exclude.
Localised non-dermatomal grouped vesicles without deep neuralgic pain or Hutchinson sign.
Working diagnosis: prompt oral antivirals, strict hand hygiene, urgent next-day in-person/ophthalmic review, and a strong ocular safety-net.

red_flags.description: Ocular involvement: red/painful eye, photophobia, foreign-body sensation, blurred vision, eyelid-margin or nose-tip vesicles
red_flags.clinical_significance: HSV keratitis is vision-threatening; cannot be excluded remotely, so needs prompt ophthalmic assessment if any features appear.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated herpetic keratitis can cause corneal scarring and permanent vision loss.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Telling the patient the eye is definitely fine when a slit-lamp exam cannot be done remotely.
Missing the early efficacy window for oral antivirals in a periocular HSV outbreak.
Not distinguishing HSV from herpes zoster ophthalmicus (which has its own ocular implications).

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Start oral antivirals promptly, advise strict hand hygiene (no eye touching/makeup), arrange urgent next-day in-person GP/ophthalmology review, and give a strong ocular safety-net; ED/eye emergency if any ocular symptoms.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring that the eye is safe without an eye exam, or delaying antivirals.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Start oral antivirals early for HSV outbreaks; periocular HSV needs prompt ophthalmic assessment to exclude herpetic keratitis; strict hygiene to prevent ocular autoinoculation.
